retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tetragonal phase can be retained at room temperature by the addition of 3 mol% yttria. Yttria-stabilized tetragonal zirconia polycrystal (Y-TZP) provides mechanical properties superior to other all-ceramic systems due to the tetragonal-to-monoclinic phase transformation. Thus, Y-TZP has been increasingly used as a core material for all-ceramic restorations. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Common Measures of Disability -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians and researchers approach disability through 3 primary paradigms. The medical paradigm is the traditional model for disability guidelines, and it focuses on pathology as the root cause of impairment. This aims to link organ dysfunction to physical limitations at the simplest level. However, this model has significant limitations, as not all organ or system dysfunctions have a definitive treatment, therapeutic end point, or a direct link to disability. In contrast, the social paradigm emphasizes the societal and functional barriers faced by individuals with impairments, focusing on identifying and addressing the accommodations necessary for their participation. This approach fosters patient empowerment and social inclusion. While each paradigm provides valuable insights, relying on them in isolation presents significant limitations, highlighting the need for a more integrated approach to understanding disability.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Issues of Concern -- Mount-Hume Classification System
retrieved_docs.content: More than 100 years later, Mount and Hume published a caries classification system following the current and more conservative approach to caries. [3] The Mount-Hume classification system incorporated the advent of fluoride and adhesive restorative materials, the use of which modified the old principles of cavity design. [3] Mount and Hume classified caries based on their site and size. [2] (see Table 2. Mount-Hume Site Classification of Caries. and Table 3. Mount-Hume Size Classification of Caries.) The size classification was updated in 2006 to include Size 0, referring to non-cavitated lesions, and modify the definition of Size 1. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine has emerged in recent years as an innovative bioactive dentine substitute. Its superior physical properties, improved handling, and various clinical applications have made biodentine popular in dentistry. It has been advocated for vital pulp therapy, root perforations, resorptions, retrograde fillings, and dentin replacement. Biodentine is calcium silicate-based cement formed by highly purified tricalcium silicate (80.1%). [1] Unlike mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA), it does not contain bismuth oxide, which is known to delay setting, affecting biocompatibility and causing discoloration. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Various classification methods for dental carious lesions have been developed, each with advantages and limitations. Newer classification systems, like the International Caries Detection and Assessment System (ICDAS) and the American Dental Association Caries Classification System (ADA CCS), have evolved from the traditional Black classification to include the current understanding of the carious process, maximize healthy tooth surfaces, and prevent extension of the carious lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Disability Determination And Impairment Rating -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: One significant component of patient care is determining a patient’s disability level and impairment rating to determine what disability services they can receive. The first step to creating an accurate patient evaluation is ensuring that healthcare professionals understand the difference between "impairment" and "disability." An impairment can be understood as any deviation or loss of body function or structure. [1] [2] Disability is characterized by limitations or reductions in the individual’s ability to participate in their activities of daily living (ADL) due to a disease or health condition. [1] [3] It is important to understand there is no definite relationship between disability and impairment. An individual may have significant impairment, such as an amputated arm, but would not be considered significantly disabled because they are still able to engage in their daily tasks successfully. Alternatively, an individual may have a minor impairment that causes significant limitations for that person’s daily activities. For example, an individual with arthritis in their knee cannot tolerate a full day of work, causing them to lose employment. Because impairment and disability vary for each case, health care providers are encouraged to consider disability in terms of a spectrum of ability to function and rate a patient’s impairment depending on how the impairment impacts the patient’s ADL participation. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Dressing Disability -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The dressing is considered an essential daily living activity, together with eating, bathing, or toileting; we can find a definition in the International Classification of Functioning, Disability, and Health (ICF) published by the World Health Organization (WHO), which allows the description and classification of functioning from a general point of view. The dressing is defined as the correct and coordinated development of putting on and taking off clothes and footwear in an appropriate way, according to the climatic and social conditions. On the other hand, disability is a term that has developed during the history until turning into a multidimensional concept nowadays; disability involves damaging in body structures or functions, capacity limitations, environment, and participation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries is a prevalent chronic disease. If left untreated, caries may progress to tooth destruction and exodontia. Dental caries is a complex multifactorial disease of individual, biological, behavioral, and environmental factors. [1] The clinical sign of dental carious is a lesion of varying severity, ranging from opacity in the enamel to frank cavitation exposing dentin. Accurately diagnosing dental caries promotes effective treatment planning, prevents disease progression, and supports optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Xenon -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Xe can be produced through the fractional distillation of liquefied air, but its high cost limits industrial use. One application is the Xe lamp—a type of arc lamp that uses Xe gas to produce an intense white light similar to sunlight. Xe lamps consist of a glass or quartz tube with 2 tungsten electrodes at the ends, filled with Xe gas after the air is evacuated. These lamps are used in street lighting, photo flashes, projectors, car headlights, and marine lighting. Xe is also used in lasers, x-ray tubes, the food industry for microorganism sterilization, and aerospace applications.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reconstructing damaged hard tissue is essential for various reasons, including traumatic or nontraumatic events, congenital abnormalities, and disease. Such damage can significantly impact orthopedic, dental, and maxillofacial surgeries. Research on numerous biomaterials shows that calcium phosphates have been utilized in hard tissue reconstruction for over 6 decades, with hydroxyapatite being the primary material used in orthopedics and dentistry.

retrieved_docs.title: Algor Mortis -- Definition/Introduction -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: After death, individuals no longer produce body heat or cooling mechanisms, and the decedent's temperature slowly approaches ambient temperature. This variable is based on the assumption that body temperature was normal at the time of death and includes both temperatures above and below normal living body temperature, 98.7 °F. Rectal temperatures are commonly used as the standard to determine the decedent temperature and algor mortis. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Lensmaker’s Equation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lensmaker equation is a mathematical computation to determine the focal length of a lens in air. The focal length of a lens is defined as the distance from the center of the lens to an optical point to which all light rays are refracted. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Occupational Therapy Assessment In Long Term Care -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Occupational therapy has sometimes been mixed with physical therapy, but in its essence, occupational therapy is an assessment and intervention to help people through the therapeutic use of everyday activities. It starts with an assessment, and then an intervention is planned to allow those to maintain, recover, or even develop activities or occupations of individuals. See Image. Occupational Therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Classification of GIC
retrieved_docs.content: GIC may be classified based on application as follows: Type I - Luting cement used for cementation of crowns and bridges Type II - Restorative cement used for aesthetic fillings Type III – GIC used as liners and bases Type IV – GIC used as pit and fissure sealants Type V – GIC used for orthodontic cementation Type VI – GIC is used for core build-up in highly mutilated teeth Type VII – Fluoride releasing light-cured GIC Type VIII – GIC for atraumatic restorative treatment (ART) Type IX – GIC used for pediatric and older adult restorations GIC may also be classified as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Assist Devices -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professionals strive to help their patients recover from illnesses or injuries to regain the ability to function independently. Patient ability levels vary as they represent a standard population, and billions worldwide require assistance when considering the definition of disability. At the intersection of disability and clinical practice, patients can still achieve independent functioning or increase their ability to function through assistive devices. Assistive technology devices (ATDs) are any systems, items, or equipment that assist individuals with disabilities in improving their daily functioning. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Imposter Phenomenon -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: While imposter syndrome is a commonly researched, experienced, and pervasive phenomenon, there is an unknown true prevalence. Currently, no formal or widely accepted medical definition exists (e.g., DSM-V criteria). Despite a lack of a formal definition, the original six criteria identified by Clance have been expanded upon since its inception and can be summarized as the following constellation of interrelated characteristics that may or may not be present in an individual with IS: the imposter cycle, perfectionism, super-heroism, atychiphobia (fear of failure), denial of competence, and achievemephobia. [1] [3] I mposter Cycle

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 5 -- Community 2922
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is recognized as a permissible element in therapeutic practice for several distinct medical conditions. Specifically, an allowed qualifier may be applied in the therapy of نوبة (seizure), فالج (paralysis), and قرحات الطرفين السفليين (ulcers of the lower extremities). This indicates that, across these diverse clinical scenarios, certain qualifiers are accepted as appropriate when planning or delivering treatment, thereby guiding clinicians in the selection of suitable therapeutic parameters for seizure management, paralysis rehabilitation, and the care of lower‑limb ulcerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: In parasitology, the qualifier “Allowed qualifier” is applicable to both قيلة مثانية and قيلة مستقيمية, indicating that these two entities are recognized and can be described using this permitted descriptor. Conversely, the term “Without” is not associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, meaning that no relationship of “associated with” exists between “Without” and these two entities. This delineation clarifies that while the allowed qualifier is relevant to both قيلة مثانية and قيلة مستقيمية within the parasitological context, the concept “Without” does not pertain to them.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the designation “Allowed qualifier” is applied within the scope of enzymology to the conditions قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, and اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder). This indicates that enzyme‑related criteria or measurements are permissible as qualifiers when evaluating these gastrointestinal and oral disorders, thereby supporting more precise diagnostic assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 5 -- Community 2956
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is linked through the relationship “enzymologie” to three specific clinical conditions: اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فالج (paralysis), and قرحات الطرفين السفليين (ulcers of the lower limbs). This indicates that enzymological considerations are recognized as permissible qualifiers when evaluating these disorders, suggesting that enzyme‑related mechanisms or assessments may be relevant in the diagnosis, pathophysiology, or management of tongue disturbances, paralysis, and lower‑extremity ulcerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is categorized as a specific type of maternal condition that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, and it is considered a subcategory under the broader classification of other conditions or status of the mother complicating these reproductive processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 784
retrieved_docs.content: Complication is linked to multiple open wounds that have not yet resulted in additional problems, and it is also associated with open wounds of the extremities. This relationship underscores that even when open wounds—particularly numerous ones or those located on the limbs—have not produced further complications, they are still considered relevant to the broader concept of complication in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2895
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure “amputation of lower extremity” comprises several more specific sub‑procedures, including بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation), بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation), and بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation). These child procedures represent distinct levels of tissue removal within the lower limb, each selected according to the extent of disease, injury, or functional requirement. Recognizing that these specific amputations are hierarchical components of the broader “amputation of lower extremity” concept assists clinicians in planning the appropriate surgical level and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 777
retrieved_docs.content: and uterine scars from previous surgeries, including previous cesarean sections, are specific indications that belong to the larger group of maternal‑care indications triggered by known or suspected pelvic‑organ abnormalities. These indications guide clinical decision‑making throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery, and they are consistently represented across Dutch and Arabic terminology, ensuring unified understanding and management of maternal health risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses a range of specific adverse‑event concepts relevant to children, and it includes the concept تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse). By incorporating this particular adverse event, the terminology provides clinicians and researchers with a standardized term for identifying, reporting, and managing umbilical cord prolapse in pediatric patients, thereby supporting consistent communication and improved patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: scarring into a unified classification scheme that supports recognition, documentation, and management of pregnancy‑related complications across multiple languages and clinical decision‑making pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2233
retrieved_docs.content: Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized under several specific types of quadruplet gestations. It is classified as حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة, indicating a pregnancy with four fetuses that has resulted in delivery. It is also classified as حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية and حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية, both describing a quadruplet pregnancy that has not been assigned to a particular care episode. In addition, Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is linked to the broader concept of حمل متعدد, reflecting that it encompasses multiple‑pregnancy scenarios. This classification framework helps clinicians recognize the various presentations of quadruplet pregnancies and their relationship to overall multiple‑pregnancy care.
